stem: A hospital uses a D-dimer blood test to screen for deep vein thrombosis. The test has a sensitivity of 86% and a specificity of 81%. The prevalence of deep vein thrombosis in the patient population is estimated to be between 5.3% and 8.1%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has deep vein thrombosis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(deep vein thrombosis | positive test) ranges from 0.202 to 0.285 as prevalence varies from 5.3% to 8.1%. Point estimate at 6.7% prevalence: 0.245.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.19.
The confidence interval [0.202, 0.285] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (86%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
